unit.name: Why generative models and how the model family tree is organized
unit.description: Motivates conditional generation through ambiguity and then classifies major generative-model families by density access and sampling behavior.
unit.summary: The main practical motivation for generative modeling is ambiguity: when one input y can correspond to many valid outputs x, a model should represent a whole conditional distribution rather than a single deterministic answer [ts=1637s] [ts=1671s]. The lecture uses language modeling, text-to-image generation, and image-to-video prediction to make this concrete, stressing that the value of p(x|y) is that it captures many plausible poems, images, or futures for the same prompt or starting frame [ts=1694s] [ts=1737s] [ts=1757s]. It then introduces a taxonomy of generative models by asking whether the model gives explicit density values or only sampling procedures: explicit-density models compute p(x), implicit-density models can sample without exposing p(x), and the tree further splits into tractable exact-density models, approximate-density models, direct samplers, and iterative samplers [ts=1820s] [ts=1843s] [ts=1912s] [ts=1940s]. Within that taxonomy, autoregressive models are the main exact explicit method covered today, variational autoencoders are explicit but approximate, GANs are direct implicit models, and diffusion models are indirect iterative samplers deferred to the next lecture; the instructor also warns that subsequent notation will often omit the conditioning variable y for simplicity even when the intended model is conditional [ts=1918s] [ts=1931s] [ts=1951s] [ts=1983s] [ts=1996s].

key_points.text: Generative models are especially useful when there are many plausible outputs x for the same input y.
Language modeling, text-to-image, and image-to-video are examples where p(x|y) captures output ambiguity.
Explicit-density models let you compute p(x), while implicit-density models let you sample without directly reading out p(x).
Autoregressive models are explicit tractable-density models, while VAEs are explicit approximate-density models.
GANs are direct implicit models and diffusion models are iterative implicit models.
